\]

The four load cells are summed (typically via a junction box whose output is the algebraic sum of the individual cell signals). Each cell has a rated capacity of 500 lb, so the combined system has a full-scale capacity of \(4 \times 500 = 2000\) lb. Because the summed output signal is linear with total applied load, a reading of 50 % of full scale corresponds directly to
\[
0.5 \times 2000\,\text{lb} = 1000\,\text{lb}.
\]
(The physical distribution of the load among the four cells does not change the result; only the algebraic sum of the forces is measured.)
Final answer: 1000 lb
questions.rationale: Total capacity is 2000 lb, so 50 percent is 1000 lb.

questions.answer:
**Parallel**

To increase the amp-hour (Ah) capacity of a battery bank while keeping the voltage the same, connect the batteries in **parallel**.

### Quick comparison:
- **Parallel**: Voltage stays the same, Ah capacity adds up.  
  Example: Two 12 V 100 Ah batteries in parallel → 12 V, 200 Ah bank.
- **Series**: Voltage adds up, Ah capacity stays the same.  
  Example: Two 12 V 100 Ah batteries in series → 24 V, 100 Ah bank.

So for more runtime (higher Ah) at the same system voltage, use parallel wiring.
questions.rationale: Parallel increases current capacity while keeping voltage the same.

questions.answer:
**Half duplex**

Standard 2-wire RS-485 uses a single differential pair (A/B) that is shared for both transmission and reception. Only one driver can be active at a time, so the bus must alternate between transmit and receive modes (typically by toggling the transceiver’s driver-enable pin). This inherently makes the link half-duplex.

(Full-duplex RS-485 requires a separate pair for each direction—four wires total—and is far less common.)

Final answer: half duplex
questions.rationale: One differential pair is shared for transmit and receive.

Mechanical wear (grooves or pits in the track) has the same effect once the wiper loses continuous pressure.
Thus the physical root cause is contamination or surface degradation of the resistive track (or the wiper itself).

Swapping the A/B wires on RS-485 simply reverses the polarity of the differential signal. In the large majority of deployments (UART-style async serial, Modbus, Profibus, etc.) this produces framing errors, constant break conditions, or outright garbage; the link stops working until the wires are restored or the transceiver is one of the rare auto-polarity-correcting parts.

The applied pressure of 11.5 psi is 3.5 psi above the lower bench-set value. Assuming a linear relationship between pressure and travel (standard for pneumatic spring-and-diaphragm actuators), the resulting travel is therefore

(The same percentage is obtained regardless of whether the actuator is direct- or reverse-acting, because the bench-set span itself is what is being normalized.)

flags: Minor overclaim: says the two 120-ohm resistors 'guarantee a reflection-free environment,' whereas real installations can still have reflections due to stubs, impedance tolerances, topology errors, connectors, or cable variation.

questions.answer:
The 120 Ω resistor is a termination resistor placed across the differential pair (A/B lines) at each physical end of the RS-485 bus. RS-485 uses a balanced, differential transmission line whose twisted-pair cable has a characteristic impedance \(Z_0\) of approximately 120 Ω. When a fast edge travels down that line and reaches an unterminated end, the impedance mismatch causes a portion of the wave to be reflected back toward the source. The reflected wave interferes with subsequent bits, producing ringing, overshoot, and data errors.
By installing a resistor whose value equals \(Z_0\) (120 Ω)
questions.reference: To terminate the line and reduce reflections
questions.rationale: Termination improves signal integrity on long/high-speed runs.

questions.answer:
A Foundation Fieldbus H1 segment (31.25 kbit/s twisted-pair) must be terminated at both physical ends to match the cable's characteristic impedance and prevent reflections. Industry standards and all major host/segment-design guidelines therefore require exactly two terminators (normally 100 Ω each) per segment—one at the host/power-supply end and one at the farthest device or spur junction. Any deviation (one, three, or more) produces standing waves that degrade signal quality and can cause intermittent communication loss.
Final answer: 2 (exactly)
questions.rationale: One at each end of the trunk.

The Modbus register holds an integer value (here, 5000). The engineering quantity is expressed in hundredths of a percent (0–100.00), so the integer must be converted to a real number by multiplication with the constant 0.01:
\[
5000 \times 0.01 = 50.00
\]
(The same relationship maps the full-scale engineering limit 100.00 onto the integer 10 000, which still fits inside a 16-bit register.)
Hence the scaling factor that is applied to the raw register contents is 0.01.
Final answer: 0.01 (raw × 0.01)

questions.answer:
The given PLC register value 0000 1010 is an 8-bit binary number. To convert it to decimal, evaluate each bit position as a successive power of 2 (starting from the rightmost bit at \(2^0\)) and sum the place values wherever a 1 appears:

- Bit positions (left to right): \(2^7\) … \(2^0\)
- Active bits: \(2^3 = 8\) and \(2^1 = 2\)

Adding the active place values yields \(8 + 2 = 10\).

(The representation is treated as ordinary unsigned binary; nothing in the problem statement indicates BCD, signed-integer, or hexadecimal encoding.)

Final answer: 10
questions.rationale: Binary 1010 = decimal 10.

A 0.1 µF ceramic capacitor placed near an IC's power pin is a **decoupling** (or bypass) capacitor. Its purpose is to supply the rapid, localized current spikes that the IC draws during switching while shunting high-frequency noise to ground.
### Why it is needed
Digital (and many analog) ICs do not draw constant current. When internal gates or output drivers switch, they produce short, high-*di/dt* current pulses. These pulses flow through the inductance of the PCB traces, vias, and package leads between the power-supply regulator and the IC pin. Even a few nanohenries of inductance produce v
questions.rationale: It shunts fast noise and supplies brief current bursts locally.

ESR stands for **Equivalent Series Resistance**.
It is the real (resistive) part of a capacitor’s impedance, arising from the resistance of the electrolyte, foil, leads, and connections. In the capacitor’s equivalent circuit it appears as a small resistor in series with the ideal capacitance.
### Why high ESR is bad in electrolytic capacitors
- **Power dissipation / heating**: The power lost as heat is \(P = I_{rms}^2 \cdot ESR\). Elevated ESR therefore produces more internal heat, accelerating electrolyte evaporation and further raising ESR—a classic failure runaway.
- **Ripple-voltage increa
questions.reference: Equivalent Series Resistance; high ESR causes heating and poor filtering
questions.rationale: A failing electrolytic often loses low-impedance behavior.

questions.answer:
The I/P transducer converts a 4-20 mA current signal into a 3-15 psi pneumatic pressure signal (standard industrial ranges). The mapping is linear, so the ideal output pressure \(P\) at any input current \(I\) is given by
\[
P = 3 + \frac{I-4}{16}\times 12.
\]
Substituting the given operating point \(I=12\) mA yields
\[
P_\text{ideal}=3+\frac{8}{16}\times12=9\text{ psi}.
\]
The transducer actually produces 8.8 psi, which is 0.2 psi below the ideal value and is therefore low.
Final answer: low (by 0.2 psi)
questions.rationale: Ideal 12 mA output is 9.0 psi.

questions.answer:
The conductivity probe uses a standard 4-20 mA output signal, where 4 mA corresponds to the lower range value (0 uS/cm) and 20 mA corresponds to the upper range value (5000 uS/cm).

The current span is therefore \(20 - 4 = 16\) mA.  
The offset of the measured signal above the lower range is \(12 - 4 = 8\) mA.  
The fractional position within the span is \(8 / 16 = 0.5\).

The conductivity value is then obtained by linear interpolation:  
\[
0 + 0.5 \times 5000 = 2500\ \text{uS/cm}.
\]

Final answer: 2500 uS/cm
questions.rationale: 12 mA is 50 percent of the 4-20 mA span.

The instrument tag FY-101 follows the ISA-5.1 functional identification standard used on P&IDs. In this scheme:
- The first letter (F) denotes the measured variable (flow).
- The second letter (Y) is drawn from the “active function” column of the standard and denotes a relay, computing, or converting element (e.g., an I/P transducer, signal relay, or flow-computing device).
Thus Y specifically identifies the instrument as a relay/computing/converting device in the flow loop.
Final answer: relay/compute/convert (ISA Y)

questions.answer:
emitter, base, collector 

The BJT (bipolar junction transistor) is a three-terminal semiconductor device whose terminals are named the emitter (E), base (B), and collector (C). These correspond to the three doped regions of the device: the emitter region, the thin base region, and the collector region. In circuit symbols the emitter is distinguished by an arrowhead that also indicates the direction of conventional current flow for an NPN or PNP device.
questions.rationale: Standard BJT terminals.

questions.answer:
The 4-20 mA current loop is a series circuit whose loop current is independent of the supply voltage (provided the supply has sufficient compliance voltage for the loop). Full-scale therefore means each loop is sourcing exactly 20 mA. Because the five loops are electrically independent (i.e., they appear in parallel across the common 24 VDC supply), the total current drawn from the supply is simply the arithmetic sum:
\[
5 \times 20\,\text{mA} = 100\,\text{mA}.
\]

CAN (Controller Area Network / CAN bus)
The CAN (Controller Area Network) protocol—standardized as ISO 11898—defines a differential, two-wire physical layer that is universally labeled CAN-High (CAN_H) and CAN-Low (CAN_L). The bus is driven such that the voltage difference between the two lines encodes the dominant (logic 0) and recessive (logic 1) states; all higher-layer vehicle and industrial protocols that inherit this physical layer (e.g., CANopen, DeviceNet, J1939, NMEA 2000) therefore also use the same CAN_H / CAN_L pair.

The instrumentation tag FFIC follows the ISA-5.1 standard for functional identification. In this scheme:

- The first letter (F) denotes the measured variable (flow).
- The second letter is a modifier; when it is also F it specifically means ratio (or fraction).
- The third letter (I) denotes the readout/passive function (indication).
- The fourth letter (C) denotes the output/active function (control).

Thus FFIC expands to a flow-ratio (or flow-fraction) indicating controller. The second F is therefore the ratio modifier.

Final answer: ratio (fraction) modifier

A 12-bit ADC provides \(2^{12}=4096\) discrete quantization levels across its full-scale input range. For a 0–100 % measurement span this yields a nominal resolution of
\[
\frac{100\%}{4096}\approx0.0244\%.
\]
Because \(4096\) is commonly rounded to \(4000\) in quick engineering estimates, the smallest detectable increment is stated as approximately \(0.025\%\).
(The exact divisor is sometimes taken as \(2^n-1\), but the difference is negligible for a 12-bit converter and does not change the approximate result.)
Final answer: ~0.025% (1/4000 FS)
questions.rationale: 100 / 4096 is about 0.0244 percent. Some systems use 4095 in practice, but the answer is effectively the same.

\[

The Pt100 RTD has a nominal resistance \(R_0 = 100~\Omega\) at \(0^\circ\)C and a linear sensitivity of \(0.385~\Omega/^\circ\)C. The temperature \(T\) is therefore obtained from the measured resistance \(R\) by the direct linear relation
\[
T = \frac{R - R_0}{0.385~\Omega/^\circ\text{C}}.
\]
Substituting the given value \(R = 138.5~\Omega\) yields
\[
T = \frac{138.5 - 100}{0.385} = 100^\circ\text{C}.
\]
(The quadratic term present in the full Callendar–Van Dusen equation is neglected because the problem statement supplies only the constant sensitivity factor.)

No, a TRIAC cannot be used to directly switch a plain DC motor circuit.

TRIACs are bidirectional AC switches that latch on after triggering and remain conducting until current falls below the holding threshold (which occurs naturally at AC zero-crossings). In a DC circuit there is no zero-crossing, so once the TRIAC is triggered it latches permanently on and cannot be turned off by gate control alone. Any practical DC use would therefore require an auxiliary forced-commutation network, which violates the “directly” constraint of the question.

Final answer: no (latching, no zero-crossing)

Because \(Z_\text{in}\) is infinite, the current that flows into either input terminal is identically zero:

The fact that ping succeeds confirms basic IP/network-layer (OSI Layer 3) reachability between the PLC and flowmeter, so the problem must lie above that level.  

Industrial protocols (EtherNet/IP CIP, Modbus TCP, PROFINET, etc.) run at the application layer (OSI Layer 7). A communication fault reported by the PLC therefore points to an application-layer issue—most commonly a protocol configuration mismatch, wrong TCP port or instance numbers, missing/expired EDS/AOP file, or an application-level timeout/security setting—even though the underlying IP path is intact.

Final answer: application
questions.reference: Application/protocol layer or device configuration above basic IP connectivity
questions.rationale: Ping shows IP reachability, not successful industrial protocol exchange.

In RS-232 serial communication, negative voltage levels (-3 V to -15 V) represent a logic 1 (also called a MARK state) on data lines. (Positive voltages represent logic 0.)
This polarity is inverted relative to common logic-level signaling (e.g., TTL), which is why RS-232 transceivers include level-shifting circuitry.
Final answer: logic 1 (MARK)
questions.rationale: RS-232 uses inverted voltage polarity compared with TTL logic.

The flow meter produces 1 pulse for every 10 gal of fluid that passes through it, and the totalizer simply accumulates those pulses. Therefore an increment of 500 counts corresponds to
\[
500 \times 10 = 5000
\]
gallons that have flowed in the one-hour period.
Converting the resulting volumetric total into a flow rate expressed in gallons per minute requires dividing by 60 min/h:
\[
\frac{5000}{60} = \frac{250}{3} \approx 83.33\ \text{GPM}.
\]
(The value 83.3 GPM is the conventional engineering rounding.)
Final answer: 83.33 GPM (250/3)
questions.rationale: 500 counts x 10 gallons = 5000 gallons in 60 minutes.

Modbus exception code **02** means **Illegal Data Address**.

This exception is returned when the client (master) requests a register, coil, input, or holding register address that does not exist or is outside the valid address range supported by the server (slave) device.

### Common causes:
- Requesting a register number that the device does not implement
- Using an address that exceeds the device's configured address space
- Off-by-one errors (e.g., requesting address 40001 when the device only supports up to 40000)
- Incorrect address mapping or configuration in the client software

questions.rationale: The requested register or coil address does not exist or is not valid.

When drawn in isolation this outline is visually identical to a capital letter “D” rotated 90°. Consequently any gate whose symbol is described simply as “D-shaped” is the AND gate, whose Boolean function is the conjunction operation
\[
Y = A \cdot B
\]
(or, in other common notations, \(Y=AB\), \(Y=A\land B\), etc.).

Because \(f_c\) is inversely proportional to \(C\) (with \(R\) held fixed), decreasing \(f_c\) requires that \(C\) be increased.

questions.rationale: Enough resistance is needed for the FSK signal to develop.

The standard (default) subnet mask for any Class C network is **255.255.255.0** (or its CIDR equivalent **/24**).
This mask is used because Class C addresses occupy the range 192.0.0.0–223.255.255.255; the first three octets identify the network portion, leaving the final octet for host addresses (providing up to 254 usable hosts on a single subnet). Any smaller mask would move the network into Class B territory, while a larger mask would create a subnet of the Class C block.
Final answer: 255.255.255.0 (/24)

  RT frames carry EtherType 0x8892 and a short, fixed header (including cycle counter, data-status, and transfer-reference bits). Because they never contain an IP or UDP header, they are demultiplexed directly in the Ethernet driver and never enter the TCP/IP stack.

- **Bypass of the non
questions.reference: By prioritizing time-critical frames and bypassing normal TCP/IP handling for those frames
questions.rationale: It keeps cyclic control traffic out of the slower general-purpose stack path.

The 4-20 mA output of a transmitter is linearly proportional to the configured measurement range. Here, that range is 50-250 °F, giving a span of 200 °F.
The measured value lies 60 °F above the lower-range value:

Dividing the offset by the span produces the percentage of span (and therefore the percentage of the 4-20 mA scale):
(60 °F / 200 °F) × 100 % = 30 %.
Thus the transmitter output is 30 % of span, or 8.8 mA.
Final answer: 30%

The output of an AND gate is high (logic 1) only when *all* of its inputs are high. With one input high and one input low, the output must therefore be low (logic 0).

Final answer: low (0)
questions.rationale: Both inputs must be high for a high output.

GSD stands for **General Station Description** (from the German *Gerätestammdaten*, or “device master data”).  

It is a standardized, plain-text device-description file (with a `.gsd` or `.gsdml` extension) that contains all the information a Profibus configuration tool needs to integrate a slave device into a Profibus DP/PA network. Typical contents include:

- Supported baud rates and transmission speeds  
- Device-specific I/O modules and their data lengths  
- Diagnostic and parameter data structures  
- Vendor and device identification numbers  
- Timing and watchdog settings  

By suppl
questions.reference: General Station Description; it tells the PLC/engineering tool the device capabilities and data format
questions.rationale: Used to integrate Profibus devices.

questions.answer:
A dashed line on an ISA-standard P&ID (per ISA-5.1) represents a pneumatic (instrument air) signal. This is distinct from solid lines (primary process piping or electrical signals) and other variants such as dashed/dotted hybrids (data/communication links) or capillary lines.
Final answer: pneumatic (instr. air) signal line
questions.rationale: Common notation for electrical signals.

questions.answer:
The small arrow/check symbol molded or stamped into a valve body (most commonly on a check valve, but sometimes on other unidirectional valves) shows the **permitted direction of flow**. Fluid is allowed to pass only in the direction the arrow points; reverse flow is blocked.

This marking is used both for correct installation orientation and as a quick field reference to confirm which way the valve is designed to operate.

Final answer: direction of allowed flow (check valve)
questions.rationale: Allows flow one way only.

\]  
Therefore the new differential pressure is  
\[
\Delta P_2 = \Delta P_1\times(Q_2/Q_1)^2 = 25\,\text{inWC}\times4 = 100\,\text{inWC}.
\]

questions.rationale: DP varies with flow squared, so doubling flow quadruples DP.

This occurs naturally at AC zero-crossings or via forced commutation in DC circuits.

questions.answer:
In P&ID/instrumentation diagrams (per ISA-5.1), instrument tags are built from a leading process variable letter followed by one or more function letters.  

- T = temperature (the measured variable)  
- Y = relay, compute, or convert (the function performed)  

Hence **TY** denotes a temperature relay, computing relay, or signal converter that sits inside the temperature control loop (e.g., a signal conditioner, trip relay, or I/P transducer dedicated to the temperature signal).  

Final answer: temp. relay/converter (ISA Y fn)
questions.rationale: Often used for a convert/relay function such as an I/P or signal converter in a temperature loop.

questions.answer:
The resistance decreases (as temperature rises).
NTC thermistors are defined by a negative temperature coefficient of resistance, which produces the inverse relationship described above. This behavior arises because higher temperatures supply additional thermal energy that frees more charge carriers, thereby lowering the device's overall resistance. (The opposite occurs in PTC thermistors.)
Final answer: decreases (inverse relationship)
questions.rationale: NTC means negative temperature coefficient.

- Coils: 0xxxx range
- Discrete inputs: 1xxxx range
- Input registers: 3xxxx range
- Holding registers: 4xxxx range
Holding registers are 16-bit read/write objects. By longstanding convention the first holding register therefore carries the human reference number 40001.

questions.answer:
The transmitter accuracy specification is given as a percentage of the instrument span (not the measured value). Convert the stated accuracy percentage to a decimal multiplier:
\[
0.5\% = 0.005
\]
Multiply by the given span:
\[
0.005 \times 200\,\text{psi} = 1\,\text{psi}
\]
Because the original accuracy figure already includes the \(\pm\) symbol, the absolute error band (i.e., the acceptable error) is therefore
\[
\pm 1\,\text{psi}.
\]
Final answer: ±1 psi
questions.rationale: 0.5 percent of 200 psi.

The closed-loop voltage gain \(A_v\) of a non-inverting op-amp is given by the standard formula

\[
A_v = 1 + \frac{R_f}{R_{in}}.
\]

Substituting the given resistor values yields

\[
A_v = 1 + \frac{10\,\text{k}\Omega}{1\,\text{k}\Omega} = 11.
\]

(The gain is positive because the signal is applied to the non-inverting input.)

Final answer: 11 (positive)
questions.rationale: Gain = 1 + (Rf / Rin).

A thicker-walled thermowell increases the time constant (i.e., slows the sensor response).
Thermowells are metal protective tubes that isolate a temperature-sensing element (e.g., thermocouple or RTD) from the process fluid while still allowing heat transfer. The measurement time constant \(\tau\) is the time required for the sensor to register 63.2 % of a step change in fluid temperature; it is governed by the lumped thermal-resistance–capacitance product
\[
\tau = R_\text{th}C_\text{th}.
\]

questions.answer:
The 4-20 mA current loop is an industrial instrumentation standard in which the minimum (0 %) signal level corresponds to 4 mA and the maximum (100 %) signal level corresponds to 20 mA.  

A 250 Ω precision resistor is the conventional shunt used to convert the loop current into a voltage that can be read by a voltmeter or analog input. Ohm’s law therefore maps the two extremes of the loop directly onto the familiar 1–5 V range:

- 4 mA × 250 Ω = 1.0 V  
- 20 mA × 250 Ω = 5.0 V  

A measured voltage of exactly 1.0 V therefore corresponds to a loop current of 4 mA, which is the minimum signal.
questions.rationale: 1.0 V across 250 ohms equals 4 mA.

questions.answer:
The HART protocol supports multiple process variables from a single field device (PV + SV + TV + QV). When a mass-flow meter (most commonly a Coriolis meter) is configured so that its primary variable (PV) is mass-flow rate, the meter’s on-board sensors also produce density and temperature signals; these are the two most common assignments for the secondary variable (SV). Either density or temperature is therefore an accepted answer; density is cited most frequently because it is measured directly by the same vibrating tubes that yield mass flow.
Final answer: density (or temp.)
questions.rationale: Either is common depending on device configuration.

questions.answer:
The standard control signal range for a valve positioner is the 4-20 mA current loop (where 4 mA corresponds to the 0% command and 20 mA corresponds to the 100% command). This gives a span of 16 mA.
Subtract the zero offset from the measured value:
15.2 mA − 4 mA = 11.2 mA.
Divide the result by the span and convert to a percentage:
(11.2 mA / 16 mA) × 100% = 70%.
Thus the measured 15.2 mA signal corresponds to a 70% command.
Final answer: 70%

**Managed vs. Unmanaged Industrial Ethernet Switches**

Both types are hardened for industrial environments (wide temperature range, DIN-rail mounting, shock/vibration resistance, redundant power, etc.), but they differ significantly in capability, configuration, and use cases.

### Unmanaged Industrial Switches
- **Plug-and-play operation** — No configuration required. You simply connect devices and they work.
- **Fixed behavior** — All ports are equal; the switch automatically handles speed/duplex negotiation and MAC address learning.
- **Limited/no intelligence** — No VLANs, QoS, port secur
questions.reference: Managed switches support configuration, diagnostics, VLANs, QoS, and similar control features; unmanaged switches do not
questions.rationale: Managed switches are preferred for critical industrial networks.

The K-factor of a turbine flowmeter directly defines the number of pulses generated per unit volume of fluid (here, 150 pulses/gal). Frequency is the observed pulse rate (300 pulses/s). The instantaneous volumetric flow rate in consistent units is therefore obtained by dividing the two quantities:
\[
Q = \frac{f}{K} = \frac{300\,\text{pulses/s}}{150\,\text{pulses/gal}} = 2\,\text{gal/s}.
\]
Conversion to the requested engineering units (gallons per minute) is performed by multiplying by 60 s/min:
\[
Q = 2\,\text{gal/s} \times 60\,\text{s/min} = 120\,\text{GPM}.
\]

questions.rationale: 300 pulses/s x 60 = 18,000 pulses/min; divide by 150 pulses/gal.

Because the op-amp is used inside a negative-feedback loop, its high open-loop gain forces the differential input voltage to zero:
\[
V_+ - V_- \approx 0.
\]
With the non-inverting terminal held at ground, \(V_+ = 0\), which immediately implies
\[
V_- = 0.
\]

questions.rationale: Feedback drives the inputs toward the same potential.

questions.answer:
The primary benefit of an opto-isolator in this signal path is **galvanic (electrical) isolation**. It transmits the sensor state via light (LED + phototransistor) rather than a direct metallic connection, so there is no electrical continuity between the field wiring and the PLC’s internal logic circuitry.
This isolation delivers several practical advantages that all stem from the same root:
- It blocks high-voltage transients, ground-potential differences, and common-mode noise that are common on field wiring.
- It prevents ground loops that would otherwise introduce measurement errors or spu
questions.rationale: It breaks direct electrical continuity and protects the PLC.

- The lower layer is 50 in of water. Because water has SG = 1.0, this layer contributes exactly 50 inWC.
- The upper layer is 50 in of oil. Because the oil has SG = 0.8, this layer contributes an equivalent water column of \(50 \times 0.8 = 40\) inWC.

Adding the two contributions gives the total bottom pressure:
\[
50 + 40 = 90\ \text{inWC}.

The standard 4-20 mA control signal is interpreted by the positioner as a 0-100 % valve position command (with 4 mA = fully closed). The span of the signal is therefore 16 mA.
At the received value of 16 mA the valve is commanded to
\[
\frac{16-4}{16}=0.75 \quad (75\,\%).
\]
Because the actuator stroke is linear, the stem must travel
\[
0.75\times2\,\text{in}=1.5\,\text{in}
\]
from the fully closed (seat) position.
Final answer: 1.5 in from closed
questions.rationale: 16 mA is 75 percent of span; 75 percent of 2 inches is 1.5 inches.

questions.answer:
An I/P (current-to-pressure) transducer converts a standard electronic analog signal (typically 4–20 mA DC) into a proportional pneumatic pressure signal (typically 3–15 psig / 0.2–1.0 bar). This pneumatic output is then used to drive valve actuators, dampers, or other final-control elements that require air pressure rather than an electrical command.
On a P&ID the device is most often shown as a small circle (or square) containing the letters “I/P”; the instrument tag itself follows ISA-5.1 conventions and is usually of the form xY-nnn (e.g., FY-101), where the “Y” suffix denotes a relay, tra
questions.reference: It converts current to pressure and is commonly tagged with the loop variable plus Y, such as TY, FY, or PY
questions.rationale: Y denotes relay/convert function; the first letter follows the loop variable.
